name: The Transformer Architecture
description: Attention Is All You Need, card by card: the encoder-decoder stacks, scaled dot-product and multi-head attention, positional encodings, and the training recipe (warmup schedule, label smoothing) behind the original Transformer. Sourced from the paper via The Annotated Transformer.

fields.front: What overall structure does the Transformer share with most competitive neural sequence transduction models?
fields.back: An encoder-decoder structure.
fields.extra: The encoder is the left half of the paper's Figure 1, the decoder the right half. Both are built from stacked self-attention and position-wise feed-forward layers.

fields.front: In the Transformer, what does the encoder produce from an input sequence of symbols (x1, ..., xn)?
fields.back: A sequence of continuous representations z = (z1, ..., zn).
fields.extra: The decoder then generates the output sequence conditioned on z.

fields.front: Given the encoder's representations z, how does the decoder generate the output sequence?
fields.back: One symbol at a time, auto-regressively.
fields.extra: In the code this is the greedy_decode loop: encode once, then repeatedly decode, pick the next word, and append it to ys.

fields.front: What does it mean that the Transformer decoder is auto-regressive?
fields.back: At each step it consumes the previously generated symbols as additional input when generating the next.
fields.extra: This property is what the decoder's subsequent-position masking exists to protect during training.

fields.front: What made the Transformer a first among sequence transduction models?
fields.back: It was the first to rely entirely on self-attention to represent its input and output, with no sequence-aligned RNNs or convolutions.
fields.extra: Predecessors chasing parallel computation (Extended Neural GPU, ByteNet, ConvS2S) all kept convolutions as the basic building block.

fields.front: What is self-attention (intra-attention)?
fields.back: An attention mechanism relating different positions of a single sequence in order to compute a representation of that sequence.
fields.extra: Contrast with encoder-decoder attention, which relates positions across two different sequences.

fields.text: Operations needed to relate two arbitrary positions grow {{c1::linearly}} with distance in ConvS2S, {{c2::logarithmically}} in ByteNet, and are {{c3::constant}} in the Transformer.
fields.extra: Longer paths between positions make long-range dependencies harder to learn, which is the Transformer's core motivation.

fields.front: What does the Transformer give up by reducing position-to-position interaction to a constant number of operations?
fields.back: Effective resolution, because attention averages over the weighted positions.
fields.extra: Multi-head attention is the paper's counteracting mechanism for this averaging effect.

fields.text: The Transformer's encoder and decoder are each a stack of N = {{c1::6}} identical layers.
fields.extra: make_model(src_vocab, tgt_vocab, N=6, ...) in the Annotated Transformer.

fields.front: What two sub-layers make up each encoder layer?
fields.back: Multi-head self-attention, then a position-wise feed-forward network.
fields.extra: Each sub-layer is wrapped in a residual connection followed by layer normalization.

fields.front: What third sub-layer does each decoder layer add beyond the two found in encoder layers?
fields.back: Multi-head attention over the output of the encoder stack (encoder-decoder attention).
fields.extra: Order within a decoder layer: masked self-attention, then encoder-decoder attention, then the feed-forward network.

fields.text: The output of each Transformer sub-layer is {{c1::LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x))}}.
fields.extra: A residual connection followed by layer normalization. Dropout is applied to Sublayer(x) before the addition.

fields.text: So residual connections can add inputs to outputs, every sub-layer and embedding layer in the base Transformer produces outputs of dimension d_model = {{c1::512}}.
fields.extra: A residual sum x + Sublayer(x) only works if both sides have the same width.

fields.front: The Annotated Transformer code applies LayerNorm *before* each sub-layer; where does the original paper apply it?
fields.back: After the residual addition (post-norm): LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x)).
fields.extra: The code comment says the pre-norm placement is 'for code simplicity'. Pre-norm later became the standard in large language models because it trains more stably.

fields.front: Why is the decoder's self-attention sub-layer masked?
fields.back: To prevent positions from attending to subsequent positions, preserving the auto-regressive property.
fields.extra: Combined with the output embeddings being offset by one position, this ensures the prediction for position i depends only on known outputs at positions before i.

fields.front: How is the decoder's mask implemented inside scaled dot-product attention?
fields.back: Scores for illegal connections are set to negative infinity before the softmax, so they receive zero weight.
fields.extra: The code uses -1e9 as a stand-in: scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, -1e9). The mask itself is upper-triangular (subsequent_mask).

fields.front: In general terms, what does an attention function compute from a query and a set of key-value pairs?
fields.back: A weighted sum of the values, where each weight comes from a compatibility function of the query with the corresponding key.
fields.extra: Query, keys, values, and output are all vectors. In scaled dot-product attention the compatibility function is the scaled dot product.

fields.text: Scaled dot-product attention: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax({{c1::Q K^T / sqrt(d_k)}}) V
fields.extra: Queries and keys have dimension d_k, values have dimension d_v. In practice all queries are packed into a matrix Q and computed at once.

fields.front: Why are the dot products in attention scaled by 1/sqrt(d_k)?
fields.back: For large d_k the dot products grow large in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions with extremely small gradients.
fields.extra: Without scaling, additive attention outperforms dot-product attention at large d_k; scaling closes that gap.

fields.text: If the components of q and k are independent random variables with mean 0 and variance 1, their dot product has mean 0 and variance {{c1::d_k}}.
fields.extra: q . k is a sum of d_k such products, which is why its magnitude grows with dimension and why the scale factor is sqrt(d_k).

fields.front: How does additive attention compute its compatibility function?
fields.back: With a feed-forward network with a single hidden layer.
fields.extra: Additive and dot-product are the two most common attention functions; the Transformer uses the dot-product family.

fields.front: Why did the Transformer authors prefer dot-product attention over additive attention?
fields.back: It is much faster and more space-efficient in practice, since it uses highly optimized matrix multiplication code.
fields.extra: The two are similar in theoretical complexity; the win is practical.

fields.front: What does multi-head attention allow the model to do that a single head inhibits?
fields.back: Jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions.
fields.extra: With a single head, averaging inhibits this. This is also the fix for attention's reduced-resolution problem.

fields.front: How are the outputs of the h attention heads combined into the final multi-head output?
fields.back: They are concatenated and projected with the output matrix W^O.
fields.extra: MultiHead(Q, K, V) = Concat(head_1, ..., head_h) W^O, where each head runs attention on its own learned projections of Q, K, V.

fields.text: The base Transformer uses h = {{c1::8}} parallel attention heads, each with d_k = d_v = d_model / h = {{c2::64}}.
fields.extra: 512 / 8 = 64. The per-head projections are learned matrices W_i^Q, W_i^K, W_i^V of shape d_model x d_k (or d_v).

fields.front: Why does multi-head attention cost about the same as single-head attention with full dimensionality?
fields.back: Each head operates in a reduced dimension, d_model / h.
fields.extra: 8 heads of width 64 do roughly the work of 1 head of width 512.

fields.front: In the encoder-decoder attention layers, where do the queries come from?
fields.back: The previous decoder layer.
fields.extra: In the code: self.src_attn(x, m, m, src_mask), where x is the decoder state and m is the encoder memory.

fields.front: In the encoder-decoder attention layers, where do the keys and values come from?
fields.back: The output of the encoder stack (the memory).
fields.extra: This lets every decoder position attend over all positions in the input sequence, mimicking classic seq2seq attention.

fields.front: In *encoder* self-attention, which positions can each position attend to?
fields.back: All positions in the previous layer of the encoder.
fields.extra: No masking is needed on the encoder side beyond hiding padding; the full input is legitimately visible.

fields.front: In *decoder* self-attention, which positions can each position attend to?
fields.back: All decoder positions up to and including its own.
fields.extra: Leftward-only information flow, enforced by the subsequent-position mask. Contrast with the encoder, where every position sees every other.

fields.front: What is the structure of the Transformer's position-wise feed-forward network?
fields.back: Two linear transformations with a ReLU activation in between.
fields.extra: Equivalently: two convolutions with kernel size 1. In code: w_2(dropout(relu(w_1(x)))).

fields.text: Position-wise feed-forward network: FFN(x) = {{c1::max(0, x W_1 + b_1)}} W_2 + b_2
fields.extra: The max(0, .) is the ReLU on the inner layer.

fields.front: Which positions does the feed-forward network mix information between?
fields.back: None: it is applied to each position separately and identically.
fields.extra: Only attention moves information between positions. The FFN's parameters are shared across positions within a layer but differ from layer to layer.

fields.text: The feed-forward network's inner layer has dimensionality d_ff = {{c1::2048}}.
fields.extra: Input and output stay at d_model = 512, so the FFN expands 4x and contracts back.

fields.front: What weight sharing does the Transformer use around its embeddings?
fields.back: The two embedding layers and the pre-softmax linear transformation share the same weight matrix.
fields.extra: Source embedding, target embedding, and the Generator's projection are all one matrix, following Press and Wolf (2016).

fields.text: In the embedding layers, the weights are multiplied by {{c1::sqrt(d_model)}}.
fields.extra: In code: self.lut(x) * math.sqrt(self.d_model). This keeps embedding magnitudes comparable to the positional encodings they are summed with.

fields.front: Why does the Transformer need positional encodings at all?
fields.back: It has no recurrence and no convolution, so position information must be injected for the model to use the order of the sequence.
fields.extra: Without them, the model is permutation-equivariant over its inputs.

fields.front: How are positional encodings combined with the input embeddings?
fields.back: They are summed, which works because both have dimension d_model.
fields.extra: Added at the bottoms of both the encoder and decoder stacks; dropout is then applied to the sums.

fields.text: Sinusoidal positional encoding: even dimensions use PE(pos, 2i) = {{c1::sin}}(pos / 10000^(2i/d_model)), odd dimensions use PE(pos, 2i+1) = {{c2::cos}}(pos / 10000^(2i/d_model)).
fields.extra: Each dimension is a sinusoid; the wavelengths form a geometric progression from 2*pi to 10000*2*pi.

fields.front: Why did the authors hypothesize sinusoids would let the model attend by *relative* position?
fields.back: For any fixed offset k, PE(pos+k) can be represented as a linear function of PE(pos).
fields.extra: This follows from the angle-addition identities for sine and cosine.

fields.front: Learned positional embeddings performed nearly identically to sinusoids; why did the authors still choose sinusoidal?
fields.back: Sinusoids may allow the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than any seen during training.
fields.extra: A learned table has no entries beyond its trained max length; a formula extends indefinitely.

fields.text: The base Transformer uses dropout rate P_drop = {{c1::0.1}}.
fields.extra: Applied to each sub-layer's output before the residual add, and to the sums of embeddings and positional encodings. The big English-French model also used 0.1 instead of 0.3.

fields.text: The Transformer was trained on the WMT 2014 English-German dataset of about {{c1::4.5 million}} sentence pairs.
fields.extra: Byte-pair encoding with a shared source-target vocabulary of about 37,000 tokens. English-French used 36M sentences with a 32,000 word-piece vocabulary.

fields.text: The Transformer used Adam with beta_1 = 0.9, beta_2 = {{c1::0.98}}, and epsilon = {{c2::10^-9}}.
fields.extra: beta_2 = 0.98 is lower than Adam's usual 0.999 default, making the second-moment estimate adapt faster.

fields.front: Describe the shape of the Transformer's learning-rate schedule.
fields.back: Linear increase for the first warmup_steps, then decay proportional to the inverse square root of the step number.
fields.extra: lrate = d_model^-0.5 * min(step^-0.5, step * warmup_steps^-1.5). The Annotated Transformer stresses that training diverges without this setup.

fields.text: The Transformer's learning-rate schedule used warmup_steps = {{c1::4000}}.
fields.extra: The peak learning rate also scales with d_model^-0.5, so larger models peak lower.

fields.text: During training the Transformer used label smoothing of value epsilon_ls = {{c1::0.1}}.
fields.extra: Same value as the dropout rate; do not confuse the two knobs. This one reshapes the target distribution, not the activations.

fields.front: What tradeoff does label smoothing produce in the Transformer?
fields.back: It hurts perplexity, as the model learns to be more unsure, but improves accuracy and BLEU score.
fields.extra: It also starts to penalize the model when it becomes very confident about a single choice.

fields.front: How does label smoothing change the training target distribution?
fields.back: Instead of one-hot, the correct word gets confidence 1 - epsilon and the remaining smoothing mass is distributed across the rest of the vocabulary.
fields.extra: The Annotated Transformer implements it with KL-divergence loss against this smoothed distribution.

fields.text: The big Transformer set a new state-of-the-art BLEU score of {{c1::28.4}} on WMT 2014 English-to-German.
fields.extra: More than 2.0 BLEU over the best previous models including ensembles, after 3.5 days on 8 P100 GPUs. The big model also reached 41.0 BLEU on English-to-French.

fields.front: How did the Transformer paper get an ensembling effect from a single training run?
fields.back: By averaging the weights of the last k checkpoints.
fields.extra: Done after training, over saved checkpoint files; no extra training cost.
